title: The Cash Flow Calculation Hidden in Your Debtors Ledger
linkedin:
Your debtors ledger has a cash flow number sitting in it that most business owners have never calculated.

The formula is accounts receivable balance divided by monthly revenue, multiplied by 30. It tells you, in days, how long your invoices are actually taking to convert to cash.

A business generating $4 million annually with $500,000 outstanding has a Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) of 45 days. If payment terms are 14 days, clients are paying 31 days late on average. The formula makes the gap concrete.

The arithmetic of improvement is direct. Reduce DSO by 10 days on $4 million in revenue and roughly $110,000 comes off the receivables balance. That money is already owed. No new work is needed to recover it. It sits in a client's bank account rather than yours.

Three patterns typically push DSO higher in growing businesses: invoicing at month end rather than at project completion, payment terms that have drifted from agreed to assumed, and a collections follow-up process that stopped scaling when the client base grew.

The timing matters right now. The receivables balance on June 30 does not arrive as cash on 1 July. With a 40-day DSO and $350,000 outstanding at year end, the bulk lands in the second week of August. July begins from the opening bank balance alone, while the superannuation payment for the April to June quarter falls due on the 28th.

A fortnight of focused collections effort, starting with the largest accounts past their agreed terms, can shift that balance meaningfully before the year closes.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness

How many days is it actually taking for your invoices to turn into cash?

There is a formula for this: divide your accounts receivable balance by your monthly revenue and multiply by 30. It is called Days Sales Outstanding, and for most owner-led businesses, the answer is longer than the payment terms suggest.

With June 30 two weeks away, the receivables balance at year end directly shapes your July cash position. A focused collection effort now, starting with the largest overdue accounts, can shift that balance before the year closes.

google:
If you run an owner-led business in Queensland, NSW, or Victoria, there is a number in your accounting software right now that most business owners have never looked at: Days Sales Outstanding.

It measures the average time between issuing an invoice and receiving payment. The calculation is straightforward. Divide your accounts receivable balance by your average monthly revenue and multiply by 30. The result tells you, in plain terms, how quickly your invoices are converting to cash.

For a business generating $4 million annually with $500,000 in outstanding invoices, the DSO is 45 days. If payment terms are 14 days, clients are paying an average of 31 days late. Reducing that DSO by 10 days releases roughly $110,000 in cash that is already owed, without any new sales.

The timing matters particularly in June. The receivables balance at June 30 becomes the opening working capital position for the new financial year. With most businesses running a 35 to 50 day DSO, invoices outstanding at year end will not convert to cash until the second or third week of July at the earliest. And July carries its own obligations: the quarterly superannuation payment falls due on the 28th.

A focused collection effort over the next fortnight, starting with the largest accounts past their agreed payment terms, can meaningfully improve the opening cash position of FY27.

The new ProfitPulse post walks through the DSO calculation, the three patterns that push it higher, and what a realistic June collections effort could release in your business.

https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-cash-flow-calculation-hidden-in-your-debtors-ledger
